svg.text: THE OLD MAN AND HIS CHAIR

div.div: Hold a loop of string across both hands. As you narrate the story of an old man at home, form string figures to illustrate each scene: two candles (a cat's cradle position), a chair (transformed by threading fingers through specific loops), scissors (another hand configuration), and a walking staff. Each figure is held up at its named moment in the story. The narrative framework helps both you and the audience track each transformation. String figure how-to guides for these specific figures are available in library books on cat's cradle.

String figure storytelling is practiced by indigenous cultures on every continent — from the Arctic to Polynesia to Central Africa — making it one of the most universal human art forms. Victorian anthropologists brought it back to Europe as a curiosity without realizing it was equally alive in their own folk traditions.

Using only a loop of string and both hands, the performer creates a sequence of string figures while telling a story: candles, a chair, scissors, a policeman's staff. Each figure is formed through specific finger loops and pulls while the narrative gives the shapes meaning and context.

svg.text: Source: The Magician's Own Book (1862) · Heritage Parlor
